summary: Shapiro demonstrates that most combination squares are inaccurate by describing a simple flip test that reveals whether the tool is truly square. He then presents three solutions at different price points: an expensive precision square, a dedicated fixed square, or a cheap rafter square made from a single piece of aluminum with no moving parts to fail. The post implicitly advocates for the simplest, most reliable tool over complex or expensive alternatives.
key_points: Most combination squares fail a basic accuracy test because plastic components wear down over time
The simplest solution (a solid aluminum rafter square with no moving parts) is often the most reliable and cost-effective
Complexity and moving parts introduce failure modes that simpler designs avoid
money_quotes: Do the two lines overlap, or do they make a skinny "V"? Yeah, thought so.
It's just one solid piece of aluminum, no moving parts, nothing to mess up.
And it's cheap enough that you can buy a bunch to scatter all around the house.
key_insight: The simplest tool with no moving parts often outperforms complex, expensive alternatives — reliability comes from eliminating failure modes, not adding features.
